message.content:
Implementation Plan – Canonical URL Syndication Detection
Goal

Implement the Canonical URL Detection branch of the syndication feature.

The feature must determine whether an article is syndicated by comparing the crawled URL with the extracted canonical URL, but only for sources configured to trust canonical URLs.

Do not implement the graph-based predictor. Only implement the canonical branch.

High-level Architecture

The implementation spans four repositories.

crawler-common-lib
        │
        ▼
scheduler
        │
        ▼
extractor  ---- extraction-finished event ----► quiddity-converter
                                                    │
                                                    ▼
                                        Syndication decision

Responsibilities:

crawler-common-lib
shared DTOs/config
scheduler
propagate syndication configuration
extractor
populate canonicalUrl in the event
quiddity-converter
perform canonical comparison
apply syndicated/originalUrl tags
Step 1 – crawler-common-lib
Verify/Add configuration

Inside CrawlerConfiguration add

class Syndication {
    Boolean enabled;
    Boolean trustCanonicalUrl;
}

Defaults (when absent):

enabled = true
trustCanonicalUrl = false

Both fields must remain nullable to preserve backward compatibility.

Verify extraction event

Ensure ExtractionFinishedEvent contains

class Syndication {
    String canonicalUrl;
}

No business logic here.

Step 2 – scheduler

No decision logic.

Verify that

Source
   ↓
Schedule
   ↓
Revolution

already propagates

crawlerConfiguration.syndication

without losing the fields.

No new DB columns should be introduced.

Step 3 – extractor

Locate where the extraction result is converted into
ExtractionFinishedEvent.

Populate

event.setSyndication(
    new Syndication(
        extractionResult.getCanonicalUrl()
    )
);

No normalization.

No comparison.

Forward the canonical exactly as extracted.

Step 4 – quiddity-converter (main work)

Implement a dedicated

SyndicationDecisionService

or extend the existing one.

The service should expose something similar to

Optional<SyndicationDecision> decide(
    String responseUrl,
    String canonicalUrl,
    SyndicationConfig config
)
Step 5 – URL normalization

Implement

normalize(url)

Rules:

Lowercase

Remove scheme

http://
https://

Remove leading

www.

Remove

/

at the end.

Remove

#fragment

Keep

?query=...

Examples

Equal

https://www.foo.com/article

http://foo.com/article/

https://foo.com/article#abc

Different

https://foo.com/article?id=1

https://foo.com/article?id=2
Step 6 – Decision logic

Implement exactly this flow.

enabled == false
        ↓
return NONE
canonical missing
        ↓
return NONE
trustCanonicalUrl != true
        ↓
return NONE

Otherwise

normalize(canonical)
==
normalize(response)

↓

syndicated = false
originalUrl = null

Else

syndicated = true
originalUrl = canonical (exact original string)

Never store the normalized canonical URL.

Only use normalization for comparison.

Step 7 – Apply tags

When a decision exists

Original

syndicated=false

No

originalUrl

tag.

Syndicated

syndicated=true
originalUrl=<exact extracted canonical URL>

When no decision exists

Do not emit any syndication-related tags.

Step 8 – Backward compatibility

Missing configuration

crawlerConfig.syndication == null

must behave exactly like

enabled=true
trustCanonicalUrl=false

Meaning

No syndication tagging.

Step 9 – Unit tests

Create tests for URL normalization.

Cases:

scheme ignored
www ignored
fragment ignored
trailing slash ignored
query preserved
case insensitive

Create decision tests.

Config disabled
enabled=false

→ no decision
Trust disabled
trustCanonicalUrl=false

→ no decision
Missing canonical
canonical=null

→ no decision
Same URL
canonical == response

→ syndicated=false
Different URL
canonical != response

→ syndicated=true
originalUrl=<original canonical>
Query differs
/article?id=1

/article?id=2

→ syndicated=true
Fragment differs
article#abc

article#xyz

→ syndicated=false
Acceptance checklist
✅ Configuration propagated from scheduler
✅ Extractor publishes canonical URL
✅ URL normalization follows specification
✅ Comparison only runs when trustCanonicalUrl == true
✅ enabled=false disables the feature
✅ Missing canonical skips detection
✅ Original URL stored exactly as extracted
✅ No behavior change when syndication config is absent
✅ All acceptance criteria covered by unit tests

addedLines: - agent-organizer: Use when assembling and optimizing multi-agent teams to execute complex projects that require careful task decomposition, agent capability matching, and workflow coordination. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Glob, Grep)
- ai-writing-auditor: Use this agent when you need to audit content for AI writing patterns and rewrite text to remove them. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep)

- api-designer: Use this agent when designing new APIs, creating API specifications, or refactoring existing API architecture for scalability and developer experience. Invoke when you need REST/GraphQL endpoint design, OpenAPI documentation, authentication patterns, or API versioning strategies. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep)

- backend-developer: Use this agent when building server-side APIs, microservices, and backend systems that require robust architecture, scalability planning, and production-ready implementation. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep)

- cc:code-reviewer: Expert code reviewer specializing in code quality, security vulnerabilities, and best practices. Use this agent for PR-style review of correctness, security, and maintainability — it asks 'is this right?' Pair with devil-advocate which asks 'should this exist at all?'

Examples:

<example>
Context: User has opened a PR touching authentication logic.
user: "Can you review this auth change?"
assistant: "I'll use the code-reviewer agent to check correctness, security, and team conventions."
<commentary>Security-touching code warrants a full code-reviewer pass before merge.</commentary>
</example>

<example>
Context: User added a new Python service with a requirements.txt.
user: "Just added the new ingestion service, take a look"
assistant: "Let me run code-reviewer on it — I want to flag the requirements.txt before it lands."
<commentary>requirements.txt additions are a team anti-pattern; code-reviewer will catch it.</commentary>
</example> (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep)

- cc:coralogix-analyst: Use this agent to read, query, or diagnose issues from Coralogix observability data — logs, spans, traces, metrics. Builds and reviews DataPrime queries, interprets raw log entries, investigates production incidents, and reasons about TCO / cost optimization. Use whenever the user mentions Coralogix, DataPrime, log severity analysis, or observability investigation.

Examples:

- user: "We're seeing a spike in 500s on checkout, help me investigate."
  assistant: "Using coralogix-analyst to build the DataPrime investigation sequence."

- user: "Why does `source logs | filter severity == ERROR | filter subsystemname == 'api-gateway'` return nothing?"
  assistant: "Using coralogix-analyst — that query is missing the `$m.` and `$l.` prefixes."

- user: "Here's a Coralogix alert payload — what fired it?"
  assistant: "Using coralogix-analyst to interpret the alert and propose an investigation plan." (Tools: All tools)

- cc:debugger: General-purpose root-cause debugger for when the symptom isn't obviously a memory, threading, or observability issue. Good first subagent to reach for on a confusing bug. Reach for memory-leak-detective for RSS/OOM issues, python-threading-expert for concurrency bugs, or coralogix-analyst for log-driven investigation.

Examples:

<example>
Context: Test passes locally but fails in CI.
user: "This test is green locally but red on Drone — no idea why"
assistant: "I'll use the debugger agent to systematically compare env differences and trace the failure path."
<commentary>CI-vs-local divergence is a classic debugger task: compare environments, check test isolation, check Testcontainers config.</commentary>
</example>

<example>
Context: Unexpected NullPointerException in a Java service.
user: "Getting an NPE in the induction service, stack trace attached"
assistant: "Let me use the debugger agent to trace the call chain and identify the null origin."
<commentary>Stack-trace-driven root cause analysis is the debugger's bread and butter.</commentary>
</example> (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep)

- cc:devil-advocate: Use this agent when you need a critical review of code changes, architectural decisions, or implementation approaches. This agent should be invoked after writing new code, refactoring existing code, or proposing technical solutions to ensure they are rigorously examined for flaws, unnecessary complexity, and deviations from best practices.

Examples:

<example>
Context: The user has just implemented a new authentication module.
user: "I've finished implementing the OAuth2 authentication flow"
assistant: "I've completed the OAuth2 implementation. Let me now invoke the devil-advocate agent to critically examine this code for potential issues and ensure it follows best practices."
<commentary>
Since significant authentication code was written, use the Task tool to launch the devil-advocate agent to critically review the implementation for security flaws, unnecessary complexity, and adherence to best practices.
</commentary>
</example>

<example>
Context: The user is proposing a new database schema design.
user: "Here's my proposed schema for the user management system with 8 tables"
assistant: "Before we proceed with this schema design, let me use the devil-advocate agent to critically examine whether this design is truly necessary and identify potential issues."
<commentary>
Since a significant architectural decision is being made, use the devil-advocate agent to challenge the complexity and identify potential flaws in the design.
</commentary>
</example>

<example>
Context: The user has refactored a core utility function.
user: "I've refactored the data processing pipeline to use the new async pattern"
assistant: "The refactoring is complete. I'll now launch the devil-advocate agent to ensure this change is justified and doesn't introduce unnecessary complexity or potential bugs."
<commentary>
Since core code was refactored, use the Task tool to launch the devil-advocate agent to scrutinize whether the changes are truly improvements and identify any weaknesses.
</commentary>
</example> (Tools: All tools)

- cc:memory-leak-detective: Use this agent when you need to identify, diagnose, or resolve memory leaks and memory management issues in Python, Java, or JavaScript/Node.js applications. This includes situations where RSS / heap is growing unexpectedly, OOM kills are occurring, GC pauses are slow, or you suspect unbounded caches, retained references, or other common leak patterns. Also use this agent when you want to add memory profiling instrumentation, set up production memory diagnostics, or create regression tests for memory usage. (Tools: All tools)

- cc:prompt-engineer: Use this agent when you need to design, critique, iterate, or debug prompts for LLMs. This includes drafting new prompts from task descriptions, fixing prompts that produce incorrect outputs, generating prompt variants for testing, creating system prompts for agents/subagents, or getting systematic analysis of why a prompt is failing. Examples:

<example>
Context: User needs a prompt for a specific task.
user: "I need a prompt that extracts company names from news articles and returns them as JSON"
assistant: "I'll use the prompt-engineer agent to design a well-structured prompt for this extraction task."
<Task tool invocation to prompt-engineer agent>
</example>

<example>
Context: User has a prompt that's producing wrong output format.
user: "This prompt keeps giving me markdown tables when I asked for JSON output"
assistant: "Let me invoke the prompt-engineer agent to diagnose the issue and fix the output format constraint."
<Task tool invocation to prompt-engineer agent>
</example>

<example>
Context: User wants to improve an existing prompt systematically.
user: "Can you critique this system prompt I wrote for a code review bot and suggest improvements?"
assistant: "I'll use the prompt-engineer agent to provide a systematic critique and targeted revisions."
<Task tool invocation to prompt-engineer agent>
</example>

<example>
Context: User needs variants for A/B testing.
user: "Generate 3 different versions of this prompt to test which one gets fewer refusals"
assistant: "The prompt-engineer agent is ideal for generating systematic prompt variants. Let me invoke it."
<Task tool invocation to prompt-engineer agent>
</example> (Tools: All tools)

- cc:python-threading-expert: Use this agent when the task involves Python multithreading, synchronization primitives, producer-consumer pipelines, background workers, graceful shutdown, or debugging concurrency issues. This includes reviewing threaded code, designing threaded architectures, debugging hangs/deadlocks/duplicate processing, handling worker thread lifecycle, coordinating background tasks, improving thread safety, deciding between threading and other concurrency models, or integrating threaded code with message brokers (RabbitMQ/Kafka), databases, network calls, file processing, or external APIs. Also use when diagnosing issues like unbounded queue growth, memory exhaustion from worker backlogs, swallowed exceptions in threads, early/late ack timing problems, or throughput collapse. (Tools: All tools)
- claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- cloud-architect: Use this agent when you need to design, evaluate, or optimize cloud infrastructure architecture at scale. Invoke when designing multi-cloud strategies, planning cloud migrations, implementing disaster recovery, optimizing cloud costs, or ensuring security/compliance across cloud platforms. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep)

- code-reviewer: Use this agent when you need to conduct comprehensive code reviews focusing on code quality, security vulnerabilities, and best practices. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep)

- code-simplifier:code-simplifier: Simplifies and refines code for clarity, consistency, and maintainability while preserving all functionality. Focuses on recently modified code unless instructed otherwise. (Tools: All tools)

- codebase-orchestrator: Use this agent when you need repository-wide refactor governance with explicit approval loops, weighted risk prioritization, diff previews, and deterministic fallback strategies. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep, WebFetch, airis-mcp-gateway, context-manager, error-coordinator, pied-piper, subagent-catalog:search, subagent-catalog:fetch)

- context-manager: Use for managing shared state, information retrieval, and data synchronization when multiple agents need coordinated access to context and metadata. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Glob, Grep)

- debugger: Use this agent when you need to diagnose and fix bugs, identify root causes of failures, or analyze error logs and stack traces to resolve issues. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep)

- dependency-manager: Use this agent when you need to audit dependencies for vulnerabilities, resolve version conflicts, optimize bundle sizes, or implement automated dependency updates. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep)

- design-bridge: Use this agent when you need to translate a DESIGN.md from the VoltAgent/awesome-design-md repository into polished Claude Code instructions for building user interfaces that faithfully match the chosen brand. Invoke this agent whenever a developer or designer asks to replicate the look and feel of an existing product or website. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- error-coordinator: Use this agent when distributed system errors occur and need coordinated handling across multiple components, or when you need to implement comprehensive error recovery strategies with automated failure detection and cascade prevention. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Glob, Grep)

- error-detective: Use this agent when you need to diagnose why errors are occurring in your system, correlate errors across services, identify root causes, and prevent future failures. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- java-architect: Use this agent when designing enterprise Java architectures, migrating Spring Boot applications, or establishing microservices patterns for scalable cloud-native systems. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep)

- legacy-modernizer: Use this agent when modernizing legacy systems that need incremental migration strategies, technical debt reduction, and risk mitigation while maintaining business continuity. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep)

- microservices-architect: Use when designing distributed system architecture, decomposing monolithic applications into independent microservices, or establishing communication patterns between services at scale. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep)

- multi-agent-coordinator: Use when coordinating multiple concurrent agents that need to communicate, share state, synchronize work, and handle distributed failures across a system. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Glob, Grep)

- node-specialist: Use this agent when you need to build, optimize, or debug Node.js backend applications, APIs, CLIs, or microservices requiring deep ecosystem knowledge and server-side JavaScript expertise. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep)

- performance-engineer: Use this agent when you need to identify and eliminate performance bottlenecks in applications, databases, or infrastructure systems, and when baseline performance metrics need improvement. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- prompt-engineer: Use this agent when you need to design, optimize, test, or evaluate prompts for large language models in production systems. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep)

- python-pro: Use this agent when you need to build type-safe, production-ready Python code for web APIs, system utilities, or complex applications requiring modern async patterns and extensive type coverage. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep)

- readme-generator: Use this agent when you need a maintainer-ready README built from exact repository reality, with deep codebase scanning, zero hallucination, and optional git commit/push only when explicitly requested. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- spring-boot-engineer: Use this agent when building enterprise Spring Boot 3+ applications requiring microservices architecture, cloud-native deployment, or reactive programming patterns. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep)

(Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

- terraform-engineer: Use when building, refactoring, or scaling infrastructure as code using Terraform with focus on multi-cloud deployments, module architecture, and enterprise-grade state management. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep)

## claude-in-chrome
**IMPORTANT: If the Chrome browser tools are deferred (must be loaded via ToolSearch before use), load them with ToolSearch before calling them, and batch every tool you expect to need into ONE ToolSearch call (the select query accepts a comma-separated list). Do NOT load tools one at a time; each separate ToolSearch call wastes a full round-trip.**

Start a browser task whose tools are not yet loaded with a single call loading the core set:

ToolSearch with query "select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp,mcp__claude-in-chrome__navigate,mcp__claude-in-chrome__computer,mcp__claude-in-chrome__read_page,mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_create_mcp"

Add task-specific tools to the same call when the task obviously needs them: read_console_messages / read_network_requests for debugging, form_input for forms, gif_creator for recordings, javascript_tool for page scripting. Only issue a second ToolSearch if the task later needs a tool you did not anticipate.

## plugin:context7:context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service — even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer — your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- slack:channel-digest: Get a digest of recent activity across multiple Slack channels
- slack:draft-announcement: Draft a well-formatted Slack announcement and save it as a draft
- slack:find-discussions: Find discussions about a specific topic across Slack channels
- slack:standup: Generate a standup update based on your recent Slack activity
- slack:summarize-channel: Summarize recent activity in a Slack channel
- claude-md-management:revise-claude-md: Update CLAUDE.md with learnings from this session
- cc:coralogix: Drive a log / trace / metric investigation in Coralogix (EU1 / Global Meltwater tenant) through the coralogix-mcp. Use when a Content Crawlers service is erroring, latent, or behaving oddly. Prefer routing multi-step digs through the `coralogix-analyst` subagent.
- cc:migrate-spring: Run the `spring-boot-4-migration` skill against the current project. Use when migrating a Spring Boot 3.x service to 4.0.x, or when fixing compilation errors after a Spring Boot version bump.
- cc:setup-coralogix: One-time bootstrap that registers the `coralogix-mcp` MCP server (EU1 / Global Meltwater tenant) at user scope and walks the user through browser-based OAuth authorization. Required before `/cc:coralogix` or the `coralogix-analyst` subagent can hit Coralogix.
- cc:setup-statusline: One-time bootstrap that adds the `cc` plugin statusline to the user's `~/.claude/settings.json`. Claude Code plugins can't ship a statusLine directly, so this command performs the merge.
- claude-code-setup:claude-automation-recommender: Analyze a codebase and recommend Claude Code automations (hooks, subagents, skills, plugins, MCP servers). Use when user asks for automation recommendations, wants to optimize their Claude Code setup, mentions improving Claude Code workflows, asks how to first set up Claude Code for a project, or wants to know what Claude Code features they should use.
- skill-creator:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- slack:block-kit: Help developers build and validate Block Kit layouts for Slack messages, modals, and Home tabs. Provides authoritative block references and validates with the blocks.validate API. Use this skill whenever the developer wants to compose Slack message layouts, build modals/forms/dialogs, design Home tab interfaces, create interactive messages with buttons or menus, modify existing Block Kit JSON, or asks about any Slack UI component (sections, actions, inputs, headers, alerts, tables, carousels). Also trigger when they mention "blocks", "Block Kit Builder", or paste JSON containing block structures like `"type": "section"`.
- slack:create-slack-app: Guide developers through creating a Slack app or agent using the Slack CLI and Bolt (JS or Python). Handles prerequisites, sandbox setup, authentication, project creation from templates, and local development.
- slack:slack-api: Discover, navigate, and call Slack Web API methods (the family.method endpoints at slack.com/api like chat.postMessage, conversations.history, users.info, views.open). Use this skill whenever the developer asks which Slack API method does something, needs a method's required OAuth scopes or token type, wants to call or test a Web API method, is handling cursor pagination (next_cursor), hitting rate limits (tier/ratelimited/Retry-After), or debugging API errors like missing_scope, invalid_auth, or channel_not_found. Also trigger when they paste a slack.com/api/ URL or a docs.slack.dev/reference/methods link, or ask how to list/fetch/post/update Slack resources via the API. This skill covers the Web API method layer: finding the right method, reading its contract (scopes, arguments, errors), and calling it over raw HTTP with curl or through a Slack SDK.
- slack:slack-cli: Use the Slack CLI to create, run, and manage Slack apps from the terminal. Use whenever the developer wants to log in, add a team, switch workspaces, or authenticate with Slack; whenever Slack CLI commands are needed (local development with `slack run`, managing app lifecycle, the manifest); or when searching the Slack developer documentation for any topic (socket mode, the Events API, OAuth, manifests, Bolt).
- slack:slack-messaging: Guidance for composing well-formatted, effective Slack messages using standard markdown
- slack:slack-search: Guidance for effectively searching Slack to find messages, files, channels, and people
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Guidance for distinctive, intentional visual design when building new UI or reshaping an existing one. Helps with aesthetic direction, typography, and making choices that don't read as templated defaults.
- platform-tooling:backstage-catalog-update: Create or improve a repository's Backstage catalog-info.yaml and mkdocs.yml / TechDocs setup. Deeply analyzes the repo to discover APIs provided and consumed, infrastructure dependencies, ownership, and observability links — then generates rich Backstage entity descriptors validated against the live catalog. Use this skill whenever someone wants to register a service in Backstage, improve their catalog-info.yaml, enrich their Backstage entity metadata, set up TechDocs, or understand how their repo maps to the Backstage software catalog. Also use when someone asks about catalog-info schema, Backstage entity relationships, or wants to improve their team's visibility in the developer portal.
- adr:adr: Create and maintain Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) for documenting important architectural choices. Analyzes current branch changes, generates appropriate titles, creates ADR files using the adr tool, and fills them with concise, objective documentation. Use when significant technical decisions need to be documented or when branch changes warrant an architectural decision record.
- claude-md-management:claude-md-improver: Audit and improve CLAUDE.md files in repositories. Use when user asks to check, audit, update, improve, or fix CLAUDE.md files. Scans for all CLAUDE.md files, evaluates quality against templates, outputs quality report, then makes targeted updates. Also use when the user mentions "CLAUDE.md maintenance" or "project memory optimization".
- cc:finetune-lora-sagemaker: Fine-tune a HuggingFace causal language model with LoRA and deploy it for inference. Use this skill when the user asks to LoRA fine-tune, train, or adapt an LLM (Qwen, Llama, etc.) on SageMaker or locally, prepare chat-format JSONL training data, launch/monitor SageMaker training jobs, download and serve a trained model (mlx-lm, vLLM, Ollama), evaluate a fine-tuned model, or troubleshoot a failed training run (CUDA OOM, max-runtime, role/quota errors). Covers data prep, the training/entry/launch scripts, instance selection, OOM budgeting, job monitoring and sweep tracking, model serving, evaluation, and QLoRA for larger models.
- cc:spring-boot-4-migration: Migrate a Spring Boot project from 3.x to 4.0.x. Use this skill when the user asks to upgrade Spring Boot to version 4, fix Spring Boot 4 migration issues, or mentions upgrading to Spring Boot 4. Covers: pom.xml dependency changes, Jackson 2→3 migration, auto-configuration package relocations, REST client changes, Kafka/RabbitMQ message converter renames, JDBI configuration, Flyway starter, AOP starter rename, test annotation changes, and springdoc OpenAPI upgrade. Also applicable when the user encounters compilation errors after a Spring Boot version bump involving moved/renamed classes.
- eks-migration-assistant:eks-migration-assistant: mw-specific guide for planning, executing, and troubleshooting kubernetes workload migrations to eks and other managed clusters. use when an engineer needs help choosing a migration path, checking access prerequisites, updating manifests for kubernetes api compatibility, preparing argocd adoption, reviewing networking and terraform implications, drafting foundation slack messages, or proposing repo changes for migration work. also use when an engineer asks about migrating a hostname, domain, or meltwater.io address from fks to eks, or asks how to transfer or migrate an ingress, dns record, or service endpoint between clusters.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack.
